name: Bacterial Leaf Blight
cause: Caused by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae bacteria. Thrives in high humidity and temperatures (25-34°C). Spreads through wind and rain.
remedy: Use resistant varieties (e.g., IR64). Ensure balanced nitrogen fertilization. Maintain proper field drainage and avoid over-flooding.

name: Brown Spot
cause: Fungal pathogen Bipolaris oryzae. Often occurs in soils deficient in nutrients, especially potassium.
remedy: Improve soil fertility through balanced fertilization (add potassium). Use healthy, certified seeds. Apply fungicides like edifenphos if infection is severe.

name: Healthy
cause: The plant shows no signs of pathogenic infection or nutrient deficiency.
remedy: Maintain current agricultural practices. Continue regular monitoring and optimal fertilization to prevent future issues.

name: Leaf Blast
cause: Fungus Pyricularia oryzae. Attacks leaves during the vegetative stage. Favored by high humidity and cool night temperatures.
remedy: Avoid excessive nitrogen application. Use resistant cultivars. Apply systemic fungicides such as tricyclazole if symptoms appear.

name: Leaf Scald
cause: Fungus Microdochium oryzae. Common in wet seasons and high humidity. Spreads via infected seeds or wind-blown rain.
remedy: Practice seed treatment with carbendazim. Ensure balanced fertilization and rotate crops to reduce field inoculum.

name: Narrow Brown Spot
cause: Fungus Cercospora oryzae. Appears late in the season. More prevalent when soil potassium levels are low.
remedy: Plant resistant rice varieties. Ensure adequate potassium levels in the soil. Practice field sanitation by removing crop residues.

name: Neck Blast
cause: Caused by Pyricularia oryzae fungus attacking the neck of the panicle. The most destructive form of blast disease.
remedy: Apply preventive fungicides at the 'booting' or 'heading' stage. Use resistant varieties and avoid late nitrogen top-dressing.

name: Rice Hispa
cause: Damage caused by the beetle Dicladispa armigera. Adults scrape chlorophyll from the leaves, leaving white parallel streaks.
remedy: Prune leaf tips before transplanting to remove eggs. Use manual insect traps. Apply recommended insecticides like malathion if thresholds are exceeded.

name: Sheath Blight
cause: Fungus Rhizoctonia solani. Favored by high nitrogen levels, high planting density, and hot, humid weather.
remedy: Reduce planting density to improve airflow. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Use fungicides like hexaconazole or validamycin if required.

name: Tungro
cause: Viral complex spread by the Green Leafhopper (GLH). Infected plants are stunted and display yellow-to-orange leaf discoloration.
remedy: Manage the leafhopper vector using resistant varieties or targeted insecticides. Practice synchronous planting to avoid continuous hosts.
